title: Dog Age Calculator — Convert Dog Years to Human Years
shortDescription: Convert your dog's age into human-equivalent years instantly.
description: Enter your dog's age in years and this calculator converts it into human-equivalent years using a common rule: the first two years count about 10.5 human years each, then roughly four human years for every additional year.
metaDescription: Free dog age calculator that converts your dog's age into human-equivalent years. Enter the age in years and see the human-equivalent result instantly.

result.label: Human-equivalent age
result.empty: Enter your dog's age in years to see the human-equivalent age.

sections.heading: How dog age is calculated
paragraphs: A widely used rule of thumb gives a dog's first two years about 10.5 human years each, then adds roughly four human years for each year after that.
This makes a 2-year-old dog about 21 in human terms, while a 5-year-old dog is around 33.

sections.heading: Why it is only an estimate
paragraphs: Dogs age at different rates depending on breed and size. Small breeds often live longer and age more slowly, while large breeds mature and age faster.
Treat the result as a fun, approximate figure rather than a precise medical measure.

howTo.name: Enter the age
howTo.text: Type your dog's age in years into the field.

howTo.name: Read the result
howTo.text: The calculator shows the human-equivalent age instantly.

howTo.name: Compare ages
howTo.text: Try different ages to see how your dog ages over time.

faq.a: It counts the first two years as about 10.5 human years each, then adds roughly four human years per additional year.

faq.q: Is this accurate for every dog?
faq.a: No. Breed and size strongly affect real aging, so the result is an approximation.

faq.q: Do big and small dogs age differently?
faq.a: Yes. Small breeds usually age more slowly, while large breeds tend to age faster.

faq.q: Can I enter a partial year?
faq.a: Yes, you can enter decimals such as 1.5 years for a more precise estimate.

faq.q: Is the old '7 years' rule correct?
faq.a: The simple times-7 rule is outdated; this calculator uses a more realistic curve.
